era_context: The mid-15th century is characterized by the transition from medieval to early modern Europe. The Hundred Years' War is in its final phase, the Ottoman Empire is expanding in the Balkans, and the Renaissance is flourishing in Italy. In East Asia, the Ming Dynasty is consolidating power and turning inward after the conclusion of Zheng He's voyages. The Aztec Empire under Montezuma I is at its height in Mesoamerica.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records in European sources)
Pacific Islands and Oceania (minimal contemporary written records)
Indigenous Americas (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically documented)

events.title: Hundred Years' War: English military decline
events.region: Kingdom of France and Kingdom of England
events.description: English military power in France continued to decline during this period. The English, weakened by internal conflict (Wars of the Roses) and French military resurgence under Charles VII, were losing territory and influence. By 1443, English control in France was reduced to a few coastal enclaves and Calais. This period marked the final phase of English dominance in France, which had lasted over a century.
events.certainty_note: English military decline in France during this period is well-documented in both English and French sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Hundred Years' War (1337–1453)

events.title: Ottoman Empire: consolidation of Balkan territories
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Murad II was consolidating its control over Balkan territories. By 1443, Ottoman power in the Balkans was firmly established, with Christian kingdoms reduced to tributary status or direct Ottoman control. This period marked the height of Ottoman expansion in Europe before the final assault on Constantinople.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman consolidation of Balkan territories during this period is well-documented in Ottoman and European sources.

events.title: Portuguese exploration: expansion of African trade
events.description: Portuguese explorers, under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator, continued expanding their presence along the West African coast. By 1443, Portuguese trading posts and forts were being established to facilitate trade in gold, ivory, and enslaved people. This period marks the intensification of Portuguese maritime expansion that would lead to the rounding of the Cape of Good Hope.
events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of the African coast during this period is well-documented in Portuguese sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Age of Exploration (15th–17th centuries)
Related to: 1444-004 (Portuguese establishment of trading posts)

events.title: Ming Dynasty: internal consolidation
events.description: The Ming Dynasty under the Zhengtong Emperor (r. 1435–1449) was consolidating Confucian orthodoxy and centralizing imperial authority. By 1443, the court had definitively ended maritime expeditions and was focusing on land-based defense and internal administration. This shift reflected the triumph of Confucian scholars over eunuch factions.
events.certainty_note: Ming Dynasty policies during this period are well-documented in official records.

events.title: Renaissance in Italy: artistic and intellectual flourishing
events.description: The Italian Renaissance was in full flourish during this period. Artists, scholars, and patrons were engaged in the revival of classical learning and the development of new artistic techniques. By 1443, figures such as Donatello, Brunelleschi, and Masaccio had already made significant contributions to Renaissance art and architecture. This period marked the height of Florentine cultural influence.
key_figures: Cosimo de' Medici (patron of the arts)

sources.name: Contemporary accounts of Renaissance artists and patrons
Vasari's Lives of the Artists (later compilation)
Modern historiographic consensus on the Renaissance

events.certainty_note: The flourishing of Renaissance art and culture in Italy during this period is well-documented in contemporary accounts and later chronicles.
cross_references: Part of broader Italian Renaissance (14th–17th centuries)

disconfirming_evidence: The characterization of the Hundred Years' War as definitively ending in 1453 obscures the fact that English claims to French territory persisted for centuries afterward. The Portuguese exploration of Africa is often characterized as purely commercial, but it was inextricably linked to the slave trade and colonial exploitation. The Italian Renaissance is sometimes portrayed as a purely intellectual and artistic movement, but it was deeply intertwined with political power, patronage, and economic interests.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1443 is moderate, with strong coverage of European political, military, and cultural events. Chinese sources are abundant for Ming Dynasty activities. However, African, Southeast Asian, and American sources are sparse or absent from European historiography. The surviving record reflects the interests of court-sponsored chroniclers, religious institutions, and Renaissance patrons. The role of non-European actors in global events is often marginalized or absent.

graph_edges.note: English military decline in France culminated in fall of Calais in 1453
Ottoman consolidation of Balkans led to final assault on Constantinople
Portuguese exploration of African coast led to rounding of Cape of Good Hope
Italian Renaissance continued through 15th and 16th centuries
